name: Fused Multiply-Add (FMA)
def: A single instruction computing a×b + c with one rounding step.
problem: Speed up and improve the accuracy of the ubiquitous multiply-then-add pattern.
mechanism: The FPU computes a×b+c in one fused operation, doubling throughput vs separate ops and rounding only once (less error).
limits: Different rounding can change results bit-for-bit
failures: Bit-level result differences breaking golden tests
Assuming FMA where the compiler did not emit it
whenNot: Code requiring bit-identical results across machines
myths: "FMA is just faster multiply-add" — it also rounds once, changing numeric results subtly
